title: Distributed Systems Lecture Series (University of Cambridge)
author: Martin Kleppmann
note: Eight free lectures (~7 hours) by DDIA's author that turn the chapter's brief 'problems with distributed systems' section into working knowledge of system models, clocks, replication, and consensus.

title: CMU 15-445/645: Intro to Database Systems
author: Andy Pavlo and Jignesh Patel, Carnegie Mellon University
note: Full lecture videos, slides, and projects showing how the OLTP internals the chapter only gestures at are actually built: storage layouts, buffer pools, indexes, and concurrency control.

title: "One Size Fits All": An Idea Whose Time Has Come and Gone
author: Michael Stonebraker and Ugur Cetintemel
note: The 2005 paper behind the chapter's OLTP/OLAP split: benchmark evidence for why a single DBMS engine cannot serve warehousing and stream-processing workloads at once.

title: Lakehouse: A New Generation of Open Platforms that Unify Data Warehousing and Advanced Analytics
author: Michael Armbrust, Ali Ghodsi, Reynold Xin, and Matei Zaharia (CIDR 2021)
note: The paper that coined 'lakehouse': details the failure modes of the two-tier warehouse-plus-lake architecture (staleness, reliability, cost) and how open formats plus a transactional metadata layer fix them.

title: Challenges with Distributed Systems
author: Jacob Gabrielson, Amazon Builders' Library
note: An Amazon principal engineer breaks a single network request/reply into eight independent failure points, making the chapter's claim that distributed systems are fundamentally harder concrete and testable.

title: Serverless Computing: One Step Forward, Two Steps Back
author: Joseph M. Hellerstein et al., UC Berkeley (CIDR 2019)
note: Berkeley's sharp critique of FaaS — data shipped to code, no addressable network state, short function lifetimes — pinpoints exactly when the chapter's serverless trade-off goes wrong for data systems.

title: Turning the Database Inside Out with Apache Samza (Strange Loop 2014)
author: Martin Kleppmann
note: Kleppmann's influential talk that shaped DDIA's derived-data thinking: treats the system of record as an immutable event log and derived data as materialized views maintained by stream processing.

title: Why We're Leaving the Cloud
author: David Heinemeier Hansson (37signals)
note: The widely debated counterargument to cloud-by-default, with real spend figures (over $500k/year on RDS and Elasticsearch alone) showing when stable, predictable workloads favor self-hosting.
